chunk_summary: Dietary diversity (MDD) and minimum acceptable diet (MAD) are key metrics for assessing complementary feeding adequacy in children aged 6–24 months, focusing on food group diversity, meal frequency, and micronutrient adequacy.
learning_objectives: Understand the definition and importance of minimum dietary diversity (MDD) and minimum acceptable diet (MAD) in complementary feeding.
Identify the 8 food groups used to assess dietary diversity in children aged 6–23 months.
Learn practical dietary recommendations for vegetarian and non-vegetarian families to meet MDD and MAD targets.
Recognize the role of MDD and MAD in reducing stunting and anemia risks in children.
key_facts: Minimum dietary diversity (MDD) is achieved when a child aged 6–23 months consumes foods from at least 5 of 8 food groups in the previous 24 hours.
The 8 food groups include: breast milk, grains/roots/tubers, legumes/nuts/seeds, dairy, flesh foods, eggs, vitamin A–rich fruits/vegetables, and other fruits/vegetables.
Minimum acceptable diet (MAD) combines MDD with age-appropriate meal frequency and minimum milk feeding for non-breastfed children.
Low MDD in India is often due to reliance on cereal gruels and dal water with minimal pulses, vegetables, or animal-source foods.
Vegetarian families can meet MDD by including pulses, dairy, fruits/vegetables, and nuts/seeds paste daily.
MDD and MAD are critical for reducing stunting and anemia risks, especially in children aged 6–24 months.
reference_quote: The updated WHO/UNICEF IYCF indicator for minimum dietary diversity (MDD) is met when a child 6–23 months consumed foods and drinks from at least 5 of 8 food groups in the previous 24 hours.
document_key_topics: Minimum dietary diversity (MDD) definition and assessment
Minimum acceptable diet (MAD) and its components
Practical dietary recommendations for diverse populations
Impact of dietary diversity on stunting and anemia prevention
